title: Worked Examples
text:
Each worked example corresponds to one textbook chapter. They are self-contained documents that walk through a realistic management scenario using R.
Use the sidebar to open the unit-specific example for any chapter from Chapter 1 to Chapter 27.

title: Worked Example 05: Identifying and Handling Variable Types
text: This dataset contains records for 300 hotel guests across several properties. It includes a range of variable types: nominal, ordinal, interval, ratio, discrete, and continuous. The goal of this example is to correctly classify each variable, choose the appropriate summary statistic, and produce a suitable visual for each.

title: Worked Example 05: Identifying and Handling Variable Types
text:
Step 4: Common mistakes to avoid



Table E5.5: Variable type mistakes and their consequences


Common mistake
Why it matters




Calculating the mean of an ordinal variable (e.g. average satisfaction).
Ordinal intervals are not equal. The mean of 'Low, Medium, High' codes is arithmetically possible but not meaningful.


Drawing a histogram for a nominal variable.
A histogram implies continuous numeric data. Nominal categories have no natural order or spacing.


Treating a binary variable (0/1) as if its mean is a standard average.
The mean of a binary variable is a proportion, which is valid, but must be interpreted as such.


Applying parametric tests to nominal data.
Parametric tests assume at least interval-level data with a particular distribution.

A colleague computes “mean loyalty tier” by coding Bronze = 1, Silver = 2, Gold = 3. Is this valid? Why or why not?

title: Worked Example 01: Describing Branch Performance Data
text:
You have been asked to review performance data for a retail chain’s 47 branches. Twelve of those branches recently piloted a customer service initiative. Your task is to describe what the data show and to identify which questions would require formal statistical inference to answer properly.
The distinction between these two activities, describing what is in front of you and inferring what is probably true more broadly, is the central lesson of Chapter 01.

Interpretation. The mean weekly revenue is GBP 4.8392^{4}. The median is GBP 4.8608^{4}. The closeness of these two figures suggests the distribution is roughly symmetrical with no severe outliers. The standard deviation indicates moderate variability across branches.

title: Worked Example 01: Describing Branch Performance Data
section: Step 3: Compare the initiative and control groups
text:
Step 3: Compare the initiative and control groups

library(dplyr)
group_summary <- df |>
  group_by(received_initiative) |>
  summarise(
    n                    = n(),
    mean_complaints_m1   = round(mean(complaints_month1), 2),
    mean_complaints_m2   = round(mean(complaints_month2), 2),
    change               = round(mean(complaints_month2) - mean(complaints_month1), 2),
    mean_customer_rating = round(mean(customer_rating), 2)
  )

knitr::kable(group_summary,
  col.names = c("Initiative received", "n",
                "Mean complaints (month 1)", "Mean complaints (month 2)",
                "Mean change", "Mean customer rating"),
  caption   = "Table E1.2: Comparison of initiative and control groups") |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"),
                             full_width = FALSE)


Table E1.2: Comparison of initiative and control groups

Interpretation. The table above describes what occurred in these two groups. The initiative group showed a change of -2.67 in mean complaints, whilst the control group showed a change of -0.6.

title: Worked Example 01: Describing Branch Performance Data
text:
Step 4: Visualise the complaint distributions

df$group <- ifelse(df$received_initiative == "TRUE", "Initiative", "Control")
ggplot(df, aes(x = complaints_month1, fill = group)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 10, alpha = 0.75, position = "identity") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Control" = "#1B3A5C", "Initiative" = "#E8A020")) +
  labs(x = "Complaints in month 1", y = "Number of branches",
       fill = "Group",
       title = "Complaint counts before the initiative") +
  theme_dmm()




Figure E1.1: Distribution of month-1 complaints by group




Interpretation. The histogram shows the spread of complaint counts before the initiative began. Both groups have similar distributions, which is reassuring. If the initiative group had started with systematically higher complaints, any subsequent fall would be difficult to interpret.
crumbs: Worked Example 01: Describing Branch Performance Data

  Statistic = c("Minimum", "Q1", "Median", "Q3", "Maximum", "IQR"),

title: Worked Example 09: Quartiles, Boxplots, and Outlier Detection
text:
Step 2: IQR fence rule

q1 <- quantile(df$asking_price_gbp, 0.25)
q3 <- quantile(df$asking_price_gbp, 0.75)
iqr <- IQR(df$asking_price_gbp)
lower_fence <- q1 - 1.5 * iqr
upper_fence <- q3 + 1.5 * iqr

cat("Lower fence: GBP", format(round(lower_fence, 0), big.mark = ","), "\n")

Lower fence: GBP 90,750 

cat("Upper fence: GBP", format(round(upper_fence, 0), big.mark = ","), "\n")

Upper fence: GBP 644,750 

outliers <- df |>
  filter(asking_price_gbp > upper_fence | asking_price_gbp < lower_fence) |>
  arrange(desc(asking_price_gbp)) |>
  select(property_id, property_type, location_tier, bedrooms, asking_price_gbp)

cat("\nOutlier count:", nrow(outliers), "\n\n")


Outlier count: 9 

knitr::kable(head(outliers, 8),
  col.names = c("ID", "Type", "Location", "Bedrooms", "Price (GBP)"),
  caption = "Table E9.2: Properties flagged as outliers",

title: Worked Example 16: Regression and Causation in Employee Data
text:
Step 2: Fit the simple model

m1 <- lm(productivity_score ~ training_hours, data = df)
b0 <- round(coef(m1)[1], 3); b1 <- round(coef(m1)[2], 3)
r2 <- round(summary(m1)$r.squared, 3)
cat("Intercept:", b0, "\nSlope:", b1, "\nR-squared:", r2, "\n")

Intercept: 45.733 
Slope: 0.661 
R-squared: 0.345

title: Worked Example 16: Regression and Causation in Employee Data
text:
Step 5: Test for confounding

m2 <- lm(productivity_score ~ training_hours + tenure_years, data = df)
compare <- data.frame(
  Model      = c("Training only", "Training + Tenure"),
  b_training = round(c(coef(m1)[2], coef(m2)[2]), 3),
  R2         = round(c(summary(m1)$r.squared, summary(m2)$r.squared), 3)
)
knitr::kable(compare,
  col.names = c("Model", "Training slope", "R²"),
  caption   = "Table E16.2: Sensitivity to confounders") |>

title: Worked Example 04: Choosing and Evaluating Sampling Methods
text:
Step 2: Simulate four sampling methods (n = 60)

set.seed(99)
n_total <- 60

# Simple random sample
srs <- df[sample(nrow(df), n_total), ]

# Systematic sample (every 10th record)
sys <- df[seq(1, nrow(df), by = 10), ]

# Stratified sample (proportional allocation)
ops_n  <- round(n_total * 300/600)
sal_n  <- round(n_total * 200/600)
fin_n  <- n_total - ops_n - sal_n
strat  <- bind_rows(
  df[df$department == "Operations", ][sample(300, ops_n), ],
  df[df$department == "Sales",      ][sample(200, sal_n), ],
  df[df$department == "Finance",    ][sample(100, fin_n), ]
)

# Convenience sample (first 60 records)
conv <- df[1:n_total, ]

label_summary <- function(samp, label) {
  samp |>
    summarise(
      Method           = label,
      n                = n(),
      pct_operations   = round(100 * mean(department == "Operations"), 1),
      mean_tenure      = round(mean(tenure_years), 1),
      mean_engagement  = round(mean(engagement), 2)
    )
}

comparison <- bind_rows(
  label_summary(df, "Population (N=600)"),
  label_summary(srs, "Simple random (n=60)"),
  label_summary(sys, "Systematic (n=60)"),
  label_summary(strat, "Stratified (n=60)"),
  label_summary(conv, "Convenience (n=60)")
)

title: Worked Example 04: Choosing and Evaluating Sampling Methods
text:
Step 4: Evaluate the methods



Table E4.3: Evaluation of sampling methods


Method
Strengths
Weaknesses




Simple random
Unbiased; every member has equal probability of selection.
Requires a complete sampling frame; can be logistically difficult for large populations.


Systematic
Easy to implement; approximately random if the list is not ordered by the variable of interest.
Biased if the list has a cyclical structure that coincides with the sampling interval.


Stratified
Ensures proportional representation of known subgroups; often more precise than SRS.
Requires prior knowledge of strata; more complex to administer.


Convenience
Quick and cheap; no sampling frame required.
Almost certainly biased; results cannot be generalised to the population.

title: Worked Example 21: Interpreting an A/B Test
text:
Step 3: Hypothesis test

n_A <- sum(df$version=="A"); n_B <- sum(df$version=="B")
x_A <- sum(df$version=="A" & df$converted=="Yes")
x_B <- sum(df$version=="B" & df$converted=="Yes")

result <- prop.test(c(x_B, x_A), c(n_B, n_A),
                    alternative = "two.sided", correct = FALSE)

test_df <- data.frame(
  Item  = c("H0","H1","Conversion A","Conversion B",
            "p-value","Decision (alpha=0.05)"),
  Value = c("p_A = p_B","p_A ≠ p_B",
            round(x_A/n_A,3), round(x_B/n_B,3),
            round(result$p.value,4),
            ifelse(result$p.value<=0.05,"Reject H0","Fail to reject H0"))
)

text: The dataset for this unit contains 200 retail transactions from a single store over one month. Suppose this represents the population of all transactions in that period. title: Worked Example 03: Populations, Samples, and Sampling Error
text: The dataset for this unit contains 200 retail transactions from a single store over one month. Suppose this represents the population of all transactions in that period. We will treat it as such, compute the true population parameters, then take samples of different sizes and observe how far each sample estimate strays from the truth. This demonstrates sampling error concretely.

title: Worked Example 03: Populations, Samples, and Sampling Error

Step 1: Compute the population parameters

pop_mean  <- mean(df$transaction_value_gbp)

param_table <- data.frame(
  Parameter = c("Population mean (mu)", "Population std deviation (sigma)",
                "Population median", "Population size (N)"),
  Value     = c(round(pop_mean, 2), round(pop_sd, 2),
                round(pop_med, 2), nrow(df))
)

             col.names = c("Parameter", "Value (GBP)"),
             caption   = "Table E3.1: True population parameters") |>

These are the parameters: fixed, true values for the population. In real work, you would not know them. Here, we know them because we have the full dataset.

title: Worked Example 03: Populations, Samples, and Sampling Error
section: Step 2: Draw samples and measure sampling error
text:
Step 2: Draw samples and measure sampling error

set.seed(42)

draw_sample_error <- function(n, reps = 500) {
  means <- replicate(reps, mean(sample(df$transaction_value_gbp, n)))
  data.frame(
    n          = n,
    mean_of_means = round(mean(means), 3),
    std_error     = round(sd(means), 3),
    theoretical_se = round(pop_sd / sqrt(n), 3)
  )
}

se_table <- do.call(rbind, lapply(c(10, 20, 50, 100), draw_sample_error))

knitr::kable(se_table,
  col.names = c("Sample size (n)", "Mean of sample means",
                "Observed SE", "Theoretical SE"),
  caption   = "Table E3.2: Sampling error across sample sizes (500 replications each)") |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"),
                             full_width = FALSE)


Table E3.2: Sampling error across sample sizes (500 replications each)

Interpretation. As sample size increases, the observed standard error falls, and it converges towards the theoretical value of sigma divided by the square root of n. The population mean is 84.91. All sample means are close to it, but none is exact: that gap is sampling error.

Non-sampling error

title: Worked Example 27: End-to-End Analysis — Selecting, Running, and Reporting Five Tests
text:
Step 3: Run all five tests

# Q1: Salary vs benchmark
t1  <- t.test(df$salary_gbp, mu = 30000)
d1  <- (mean(df$salary_gbp) - 30000) / sd(df$salary_gbp)

# Q2: Training effect on productivity
yes <- df$productivity[df$training_completed == "Yes"]
no  <- df$productivity[df$training_completed == "No"]
t2  <- t.test(yes, no, var.equal = FALSE)
sp  <- sqrt(((length(yes)-1)*var(yes) + (length(no)-1)*var(no)) /
              (length(yes)+length(no)-2))
d2  <- (mean(yes) - mean(no)) / sp

# Q3: Productivity by department
anova_m <- aov(productivity ~ department, data = df)
a_tbl   <- summary(anova_m)[[1]]
eta2    <- a_tbl$`Sum Sq`[1] / sum(a_tbl$`Sum Sq`)

# Q4: Engagement by turnover intention
mw <- wilcox.test(engagement_score ~ turnover_intention,
                   data = df, exact = FALSE)

# Q5: Training vs satisfaction band
ct  <- table(df$training_completed, df$satisfaction_band)
chi <- chisq.test(ct, correct = FALSE)
V   <- sqrt(chi$statistic / (nrow(df) * min(nrow(ct)-1, ncol(ct)-1)))

title: Worked Example 23: Independent and Paired t-Tests — Sales Training
section: Step 3: Independent samples t-test (Team A vs B, post-training)
text:
Step 3: Independent samples t-test (Team A vs B, post-training)

A_after <- df$score_after[df$team == "A"]
B_after <- df$score_after[df$team == "B"]
ind     <- t.test(A_after, B_after, var.equal = FALSE)
n_A <- length(A_after); n_B <- length(B_after)
s_p <- sqrt(((n_A-1)*var(A_after) + (n_B-1)*var(B_after)) / (n_A+n_B-2))
d_ind <- (mean(A_after) - mean(B_after)) / s_p

ind_out <- data.frame(
  Element = c("Mean Team A","Mean Team B","Difference (A-B)",
              "t","df","p-value","Cohen's d"),
  Value = round(c(mean(A_after), mean(B_after),
                  mean(A_after)-mean(B_after),
                  ind$statistic, ind$parameter,
                  ind$p.value, d_ind), 3)
)
knitr::kable(ind_out, col.names = c("Element","Value"),
  caption = "Table E23.2: Independent t-test — post-training scores") |>

title: Worked Example 25: Chi-Square Test — Segment and Channel Association
text:
Step 3: Chi-square test of independence

chi_result <- chisq.test(ct, correct = FALSE)
n <- sum(ct)
k <- min(nrow(ct)-1, ncol(ct)-1)
V <- sqrt(chi_result$statistic / (n * k))

chi_out <- data.frame(
  Element = c("H0","H1","Chi-squared","df","p-value",
              "Cramér's V","Interpretation"),
  Value = c("Segment and channel are independent",
            "Segment and channel are associated",
            round(chi_result$statistic, 3),
            chi_result$parameter,
            round(chi_result$p.value, 4),
            round(V, 3),
            ifelse(V < 0.1,"Negligible",
                   ifelse(V < 0.3,"Small",
                          ifelse(V < 0.5,"Moderate","Strong"))))
)
knitr::kable(chi_out, col.names = c("Element","Value"),
  caption = "Table E25.3: Chi-square test results") |>

title: Worked Example 03: Populations, Samples, and Sampling Error
text: The dataset for this unit contains 200 retail transactions from a single store over one month. Suppose this represents the population of all transactions in that period. title: Worked Example 03: Populations, Samples, and Sampling Error

Step 1: Compute the population parameters

pop_mean  <- mean(df$transaction_value_gbp)
pop_sd    <- sd(df$transaction_value_gbp)
pop_med   <- median(df$transaction_value_gbp)

param_table <- data.frame(
  Parameter = c("Population mean (mu)", "Population std deviation (sigma)",
                "Population median", "Population size (N)"),
  Value     = c(round(pop_mean, 2), round(pop_sd, 2),
                round(pop_med, 2), nrow(df))
)
knitr::kable(param_table,
             col.names = c("Parameter", "Value (GBP)"),
             caption   = "Table E3.1: True population parameters") |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped"), full_width = FALSE)

These are the parameters: fixed, true values for the population. In real work, you would not know them. Here, we know them because we have the full dataset.

title: Worked Example 03: Populations, Samples, and Sampling Error
section: Step 2: Draw samples and measure sampling error
text:
Step 2: Draw samples and measure sampling error

set.seed(42)

draw_sample_error <- function(n, reps = 500) {
  means <- replicate(reps, mean(sample(df$transaction_value_gbp, n)))
  data.frame(
    n          = n,
    mean_of_means = round(mean(means), 3),
    std_error     = round(sd(means), 3),
    theoretical_se = round(pop_sd / sqrt(n), 3)
  )
}

se_table <- do.call(rbind, lapply(c(10, 20, 50, 100), draw_sample_error))

knitr::kable(se_table,
  col.names = c("Sample size (n)", "Mean of sample means",
                "Observed SE", "Theoretical SE"),
  caption   = "Table E3.2: Sampling error across sample sizes (500 replications each)") |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"),
                             full_width = FALSE)

Interpretation. As sample size increases, the observed standard error falls, and it converges towards the theoretical value of sigma divided by the square root of n. The population mean is 84.91. All sample means are close to it, but none is exact: that gap is sampling error.

Table E3.3: Sampling error vs non-sampling error


Error type
Source
How to reduce it




Sampling error
Random variation in which cases happen to be selected.
Increasing the sample size.


Non-sampling error
Measurement error, recording mistakes, non-response, or a biased sampling frame.
Improving data collection procedures; cannot be reduced by a larger sample.

title: Worked Example 04: Choosing and Evaluating Sampling Methods
text:
Step 2: Simulate four sampling methods (n = 60)

set.seed(99)
n_total <- 60

# Simple random sample
srs <- df[sample(nrow(df), n_total), ]

# Systematic sample (every 10th record)
sys <- df[seq(1, nrow(df), by = 10), ]

# Stratified sample (proportional allocation)
ops_n  <- round(n_total * 300/600)
sal_n  <- round(n_total * 200/600)
fin_n  <- n_total - ops_n - sal_n
strat  <- bind_rows(
  df[df$department == "Operations", ][sample(300, ops_n), ],
  df[df$department == "Sales",      ][sample(200, sal_n), ],
  df[df$department == "Finance",    ][sample(100, fin_n), ]
)

# Convenience sample (first 60 records)
conv <- df[1:n_total, ]

label_summary <- function(samp, label) {
  samp |>
    summarise(
      Method           = label,
      n                = n(),
      pct_operations   = round(100 * mean(department == "Operations"), 1),
      mean_tenure      = round(mean(tenure_years), 1),
      mean_engagement  = round(mean(engagement), 2)
    )
}

comparison <- bind_rows(
  label_summary(df, "Population (N=600)"),
  label_summary(srs, "Simple random (n=60)"),
  label_summary(sys, "Systematic (n=60)"),
  label_summary(strat, "Stratified (n=60)"),
  label_summary(conv, "Convenience (n=60)")
)

knitr::kable(comparison,
  col.names = c("Method", "n", "% Operations",
                "Mean tenure (yrs)", "Mean engagement"),
  caption   = "Table E4.2: Comparison of sampling methods against population") |>

title: Worked Example 04: Choosing and Evaluating Sampling Methods
text:
Step 4: Evaluate the methods



Table E4.3: Evaluation of sampling methods


Method
Strengths
Weaknesses




Simple random
Unbiased; every member has equal probability of selection.
Requires a complete sampling frame; can be logistically difficult for large populations.


Systematic
Easy to implement; approximately random if the list is not ordered by the variable of interest.
Biased if the list has a cyclical structure that coincides with the sampling interval.


Stratified
Ensures proportional representation of known subgroups; often more precise than SRS.
Requires prior knowledge of strata; more complex to administer.


Convenience
Quick and cheap; no sampling frame required.
Almost certainly biased; results cannot be generalised to the population.

The convenience sample uses the first 60 records. Why might this be a particularly poor strategy with this dataset?
If you could only ask 30 questions in a staff survey, which sampling method would you recommend? Justify your answer.

title: Worked Example 10: The Normal Curve and Z-Scores
text:
Step 2: Apply the empirical rule

mu <- mean(df$statistics_score)
s  <- sd(df$statistics_score)

emp_check <- data.frame(
  Rule     = c("1 SD", "2 SD", "3 SD"),
  Expected = c("~68%", "~95%", "~99.7%"),
  Actual   = round(100 * c(
    mean(df$statistics_score >= mu - s   & df$statistics_score <= mu + s),
    mean(df$statistics_score >= mu - 2*s & df$statistics_score <= mu + 2*s),
    mean(df$statistics_score >= mu - 3*s & df$statistics_score <= mu + 3*s)
  ), 1)
)
emp_check$Actual <- paste0(emp_check$Actual, "%")

title: Worked Example 01: Describing Branch Performance Data
text:
You have been asked to review performance data for a retail chain’s 47 branches. Twelve of those branches recently piloted a customer service initiative. Your task is to describe what the data show and to identify which questions would require formal statistical inference to answer properly.
The distinction between these two activities, describing what is in front of you and inferring what is probably true more broadly, is the central lesson of Chapter 01.

section: Step 3: Compare the initiative and control groups
text:
Step 3: Compare the initiative and control groups

library(dplyr)
group_summary <- df |>
  group_by(received_initiative) |>
  summarise(
    n                    = n(),
    mean_complaints_m1   = round(mean(complaints_month1), 2),
    mean_complaints_m2   = round(mean(complaints_month2), 2),
    change               = round(mean(complaints_month2) - mean(complaints_month1), 2),
    mean_customer_rating = round(mean(customer_rating), 2)
  )

knitr::kable(group_summary,
  col.names = c("Initiative received", "n",
                "Mean complaints (month 1)", "Mean complaints (month 2)",
                "Mean change", "Mean customer rating"),
  caption   = "Table E1.2: Comparison of initiative and control groups") |>

title: Worked Example 01: Describing Branch Performance Data
text:
Step 4: Visualise the complaint distributions

df$group <- ifelse(df$received_initiative == "TRUE", "Initiative", "Control")
ggplot(df, aes(x = complaints_month1, fill = group)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 10, alpha = 0.75, position = "identity") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Control" = "#1B3A5C", "Initiative" = "#E8A020")) +
  labs(x = "Complaints in month 1", y = "Number of branches",
       fill = "Group",
       title = "Complaint counts before the initiative") +
  theme_dmm()




Figure E1.1: Distribution of month-1 complaints by group




Interpretation. The histogram shows the spread of complaint counts before the initiative began. Both groups have similar distributions, which is reassuring. If the initiative group had started with systematically higher complaints, any subsequent fall would be difficult to interpret.

title: Worked Example 01: Describing Branch Performance Data
section: Step 5: What these data can and cannot tell us
text:
Step 5: What these data can and cannot tell us



Table E1.3: What description can and cannot address


Question
Type
Answerable from this analysis?




What was the average weekly revenue across our 47 branches?
Descriptive
Yes


Did the initiative group have fewer complaints in month 2?
Descriptive
Yes


Did the initiative cause the change in complaints?
Cannot determine from description alone
No


Would this initiative work in branches outside our dataset?
Inferential
Needs inference


Is the difference in complaints statistically significant?
Inferential
Needs a formal test

title: Worked Example 01: Describing Branch Performance Data
text:
Reflection questions

Why is it incorrect to say “the initiative reduced complaints” based only on the descriptive comparison above?
What additional analysis would be required to make a defensible causal claim?
If you were presenting Table E1.2 to a senior manager, what caveats would you include?

title: Worked Example 05: Identifying and Handling Variable Types
text:
Step 4: Common mistakes to avoid



Table E5.5: Variable type mistakes and their consequences


Common mistake
Why it matters




Calculating the mean of an ordinal variable (e.g. average satisfaction).
Ordinal intervals are not equal. The mean of 'Low, Medium, High' codes is arithmetically possible but not meaningful.


Drawing a histogram for a nominal variable.
A histogram implies continuous numeric data. Nominal categories have no natural order or spacing.


Treating a binary variable (0/1) as if its mean is a standard average.
The mean of a binary variable is a proportion, which is valid, but must be interpreted as such.


Applying parametric tests to nominal data.
Parametric tests assume at least interval-level data with a particular distribution.
